segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [slowly] Chapter Ten: What Torvald Knows

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Torvald listened the way the city's bowl caught sound — without effort, without interrupting, letting everything settle to the floor.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass talked for a long time. He laid out the inspection report, the language about

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [softly] fitness,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Section Twelve and what it triggered, the old-alloy stock his father knew was insufficient and had known for years. Then the co-custodial clause, which he had to explain twice because his voice went flat the first time and he wasn't sure he'd said it in the right order. Then the lame chord — the way Perin's ratification note had sat a quarter-tone low, the specific texture of it, greasy and unresolved, the kind of thing you'd hear if you were listening for it and miss entirely if you weren't.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Torvald's expression didn't change. He cupped his left ear slightly and kept his eyes on Cass.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The sub-harmonic in the bells took the longest to explain. Cass had not said it aloud to anyone before — not the full version, not the part about how it felt like a question rather than a sound, how it was lowest on mornings when his father's hands shook worst at breakfast and then settled by the time they reached the workshop. Saying it to Torvald's face, in the Compact's back room with its smell of old dye and pine resin, felt like striking a test note against a practice bell he'd never tuned. He didn't know what the resonance would be.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He stopped talking.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Torvald was quiet. On the Dyer's Road, a cart horse shifted its weight and one of the wheel-spokes knocked against the cobble. Past the Fade Line, that sound was just a sound.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: The inspection was today,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass said, because the silence had started to feel like something he'd broken.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: Yes,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Torvald said.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: And.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: I heard you.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He stood up, crossed to the window, looked out at nothing in particular. His hands, blue-green from thirty years of dye work, hung at his sides. He turned back after a moment.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: You're describing a system that — look, it's like a dye that doesn't stain. You dip the cloth and it comes out changed but there's nothing on your hands, nothing in the vat, no trace of the color anywhere except in the fabric itself. Coercion without fingerprints.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He said it the way Cass said

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [softly] that fifth is flat

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: — as an observation, a thing the ear simply caught.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: I've been saying that for twenty years, in one way or another, to anyone who'd sit still long enough. Nobody listened because I don't have credentials and because a retired dye-merchant talking about contract law sounds about as serious as a bell-maker giving advice on indigo.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The under-note had been with him since the inspection; it hadn't gone anywhere.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: The report uses the word

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: [softly] fitness,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass said.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: Section Four of the custodianship agreement. Yes. She's had this ready for a while.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Torvald came back to the table and sat down heavily.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: The question is what you do with it. Not what she's done. What

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: [firmly] you

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: do.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: I've been trying to find a mechanism inside the Codex. Something that would void the clause, or contest the inspection findings —

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: I know what you've been trying.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Torvald's voice was not unkind.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: The system just moved against you. That's the part you have to sit with for a minute.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: So what else is there.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Torvald almost smiled. He reached under the table and produced a flat wooden box, the kind used for keeping tally-songs — notched records, strips of scored wood that the Academy didn't recognize and the Compact relied on completely. He didn't open it. He set it in front of him and pressed both palms on the lid.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: There's a group,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: he said.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: Informal. No name they use publicly. I call them the Blank Pages Society because that's what they care about — the back of Volume Four.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass had heard of the blank pages. Every Academy student had. Forty-three of them, sitting at the end of the volume that was supposed to cover exceptional circumstances and contested frequencies. The official explanation was that they were reserved for future additions. The official explanation had been the official explanation for a hundred and sixty years.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: Scholars,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Torvald said.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: A few frustrated Court Singers. People who think the blank pages are deliberate — that someone left them empty on purpose and the reason for it matters. I know two of them personally.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He paused.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: One is a retired lecturer. Taught acoustical theory at the Academy for thirty years before they put her out. Her name is Mira Fen.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass heard the name and something in his chest did a strange, short movement.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Proctor Fen's mother.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: Yes.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He almost laughed. He stopped himself, but the shape of it was there — the specific, wrong-key humor of finding out that the proctor who had docked his marks on a contract theory exercise last spring was the son of the woman who thought the whole system had a gap in it. , but it gave the laugh somewhere to go.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Who's the other one,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: he said.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: [slowly] Dav Sorn.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The name landed wrong. Cass turned it over, checked the pitch of it, checked again.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: The Court Singer.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: He's been attending meetings for three years. Hasn't acted on anything they've discussed.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Torvald spread his hands briefly — not a shrug, just an acknowledgment of the shape of the thing.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: He's there. That's not nothing.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Dav Sorn's voice in a courtroom — the precise, hedged sentences, the way he touched his throat when he was working through something.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [softly] In my assessment. Based on available evidence.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: A man who had composed forty-three verdicts and been satisfied with eleven of them. Sitting in a room with scholars who believed the system had a deliberate hole in it. For three years. Doing nothing.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He didn't say anything. The thought sat in his chest.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: The Corda woman,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Torvald said.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: She knows something about the bells that she's been using as pressure. Not the inspection — that's new, that's the mechanism she chose to use the pressure with. The pressure itself is older. She's had it for thirty years.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: The journals,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass said.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: Whatever's in them. Yes.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: They're in the locked room.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: Yes.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: My father has the key.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: Yes.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Torvald's voice was level. He let the silence run for a moment.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: I'm not telling you to steal from your father, boy. I'm telling you the answer is in that room.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [slowly] The difference between those two statements was very small. Cass could hear it — the gap between them, a quarter-tone at most, the kind of interval that looked clean on paper and sat wrong in the air.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He nodded once.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The door opened.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Lenne came in carrying a fold of paper in one hand and her coat in the other, the coat still half-on because she'd been moving quickly and hadn't stopped to sort herself out. She looked at Torvald, then at Cass, then at Torvald again.

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: You started without me,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: she said.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: You were late.

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: I was gathering information.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: She dropped into the chair across from Cass and got her second arm into the coat sleeve with the focused efficiency of someone who has already moved on from the coat problem. She put the paper on the table.

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: My mother was approached.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass went still.

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: The House of Corda. They sent a representative to the Ashvale house four days ago. Asked her to ratify the co-custodial transfer — the bells moving from Bellwright custody to joint Corda-Bellwright management.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: She said it the way she said everything: precisely, with the Academy's careful diction, but underneath it something that wasn't quite level.

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: She refused.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Good,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass said.

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: [worried] She's now under pressure.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Lenne's finger moved on the table — a small, unconscious gesture, fingering an interval she wasn't playing.

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: The Corda-retained Court Singers filed a complaint with the Academy two days ago. About a verdict my mother sang in the Merchant Quarter last spring. They're claiming the interval resolution was irregular.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The spring verdict. Cass remembered Lenne saying something about it months ago —

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [softly] I watched. The form was perfect. I've been thinking about it since.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He looked at her face. Her jaw was set.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Was it?

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: he said.

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: [firmly] No.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Short. Certain.

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: The form was correct. I was there. I watched her tune the ratification note against the reference bell before she sang a single phrase.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: She picked up the paper and put it down again.

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: The complaint is — it's a message. That's all it is.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: Does your mother know you're here?

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Torvald said.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: A pause.

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: [hesitantly] She told me to stay out of it.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: And.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Lenne looked at him.

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: [firmly] I'm here.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Torvald made a sound that was not quite approval and not quite anything else. He leaned back in his chair and the old wood creaked under him.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: [tense] The pressure is spreading,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: he said, to both of them and neither of them specifically.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: That's what this is. The Corda woman has the inspection mechanism ready, she's moving on the bells, and anyone who might complicate that process is getting a message. The Ashvale complaint is the message.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: What do we do about it,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass said.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: Nothing yet. Vella Ashvale is the best Court Singer in this city and she knows how to handle a complaint. That's not your problem right now.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He looked at Cass.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: Your problem is the locked room.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Lenne looked at Cass.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He didn't explain. She didn't ask. That was one of the things about Lenne — she gathered information and waited to see if it was offered, and if it wasn't, she filed it and moved on. He'd noticed it early and found it useful in a way he hadn't expected.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: They stayed another half-hour. Torvald talked through what Mira Fen had published — three papers, none of them through the Academy, all of them circulated in the Compact's network — about the blank pages. The argument, roughly, was that Volume Four's exceptional-circumstances section had been left incomplete because completing it would require acknowledging that the system couldn't account for coercion. That the forty-three blank pages were a gap that someone had decided was better left unfilled than filled honestly.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: She can't prove it,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Torvald said.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: She has the structural argument. She needs the source material.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: The journals,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass said.

segments.speaker: TORVALD
segments.text: The journals.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The under-note had settled to a dull, persistent weight by the time they left. Outside, the Dyer's Road had gone to evening light — amber off the limestone, long shadows from the vat-buildings, the smell of indigo and something chemical underneath it that caught at the back of the throat. Lenne walked with him as far as the Fade Line.

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: Mira Fen,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: she said, when they were nearly there.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Yes.

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: She's been to our house. My mother knows her.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: She paused.

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: I didn't know about the Society.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: Torvald said your mother knows things.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Lenne was quiet for a moment.

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: She knows more than she says. That's not — I mean, I've known that for a while. She watches my father's verdicts and doesn't say anything.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: She stopped walking. They were at the Fade Line now, the invisible boundary where the city's careful acoustics began to take hold. On this side, the cart horse and the dye-vat sounds and the normal noise of a district that talked out loud. On the other side, the lower, considered murmur of the inner city, where you kept your voice down because voices carried.

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: I've been thinking about the spring verdict,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: she said.

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: The Merchant Quarter one.

segments.speaker: CASS
segments.text: You said the form was perfect.

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: [slowly] It was. That's the part I can't stop thinking about.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: She looked at him — not asking him to solve it, just saying it to someone who would hear it.

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: The form was correct and the outcome was wrong. I watched the whole thing. I know the intervals. And the outcome was wrong.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He didn't say anything. She wasn't asking him to.

segments.speaker: LENNE
segments.text: Good luck,

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: she said. Not what she meant, and they both knew it, and she turned back toward the Weavers' District while he crossed the Fade Line and walked home through a city that had gone quiet for the evening.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [pause]

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The house was lit when he got there. His father's shadow moved behind the workshop's high window — still working, the Civic Pitch long done, the bells for the day all struck and settled. Cass stood in the narrow passage between the workshop wall and the kitchen door and looked up at the shadow for a moment. Then he went inside.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He ate the bread and cold soup his mother had left covered on the kitchen table. The kitchen was quiet. The wall to the locked room was two feet from where he sat, plain plaster, a door in it that had been locked since he was four years old and that he had never once tried to open.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He had always told himself there was a reason he'd never tried. That his father would tell him when he was ready, when the time was right, when Cass had learned enough to understand what was in there.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He was fourteen years old.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [slowly] His brother had been in service for ten years, composing contract music for the House of Corda in a resonance chamber with acoustics better than the Academy practice rooms, writing letters about the weather and the quality of the bells and what he'd had for lunch. His father's hands shook at the breakfast table and settled at the lathe and had been doing both for ten years. The bells were going to be taken, or shared, or transferred, or whatever word the co-custodial clause used to describe the thing that was happening. The Corda woman had a document and a mechanism and thirty years of pressure built from something she'd read in journals that sat twenty feet from where Cass was eating cold soup.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: His father had not told him anything.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [slowly] He was ready.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He set the bowl down. The sound of it on the table was ordinary and specific — ceramic on wood, a small resonance that died in less than a second. He pressed his palm flat on the table beside the bowl and felt the grain of the wood under his hand, the slight roughness of it, the temperature.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: The bells in the locked room were old — three of them, small, hung from iron brackets, cast from the same alloy as the forty-seven originals. He had never heard them ring. He had stood close to the door sometimes, in the kitchen, and heard nothing from the other side, which meant either the room was acoustically sealed or the bells were silent. He had always assumed silent.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He was no longer sure he'd assumed correctly.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: There was a pitch the body knew before the ear did. He had felt it his whole life, in the mornings when the Civic Pitch sounded, in the specific harmonic that sat beneath the sequence like a current under still water — not painful, not quite pleasure, something older than either.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He was still afraid. He was going to open the room anyway.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: His father kept spares of everything. Organized by type, on a board by the lathe — workshop keys on the left side, house keys on the right, each one on a labeled hook in Eddan's careful hand. Cass had grown up watching him hang them there after use, take them down before work, replace them exactly. It was the most orderly thing about him. The rest of the workshop was controlled chaos, bronze filings in the floor grit, bells in progress crowding the iron frames, half-finished clappers on every horizontal surface. But the key board was precise.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He had seen the locked room's key on that board. He had seen it for years, a small brass thing on a hook labeled

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [softly] K. rm.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: in his father's shorthand, and he had never taken it down because he had told himself there was a reason he was waiting.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He was done waiting.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He got up from the table. He rinsed the bowl at the basin and set it to dry. He went to bed, because his father was still in the workshop and there was no point going for the key with his father twenty feet away and the night still early. He lay in the dark and listened to the workshop sounds — the low rasp of the lathe, a test strike against a practice bell, the clean note of it ringing through the wall and then fading.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He didn't sleep for a long time.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [tense] Sometime past midnight, when the workshop had been silent for hours, he heard movement below. Not the workshop — the kitchen. The particular creak of the floorboard near the basin, then the heavier one by the locked room door. His father's tread, unmistakable even through the ceiling, the weight of him shifting from one foot to the other.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Then the sound of a key in a lock.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass held still. The door opened — he couldn't hear it open, but he heard the change in the house's acoustics, the way the air moved differently when that room was unsealed. Then nothing for a long time. Long enough that Cass thought his father might have gone in and closed the door behind him.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: But then: a page turning. Not one page — several, in quick succession, the sound of someone flipping through something they'd read before, looking for a specific passage. The rustle stopped. A pause. Then his father's breathing, audible through the ceiling in the dead-quiet house, the long uneven exhale of a man reading something he already knew by heart and reading it again anyway.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: It went on for ten minutes. Maybe fifteen. Then the pages again, slower this time. The door. The lock. The creak of the kitchen floorboard, and then the stairs, and then his father passing his door without stopping, the tremor in his footsteps that the lathe couldn't steady.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: Cass lay in the dark and breathed.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: When he finally did, he dreamed of nothing. Or if he dreamed of something, he didn't remember it. What he remembered, waking in the gray early morning before the Civic Pitch, was the specific feeling of having made a decision — the clean, settled fact of it, sitting in his chest like a struck bell that had finally stopped vibrating.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: [slowly] He was going to open the room.

segments.speaker: NARRATOR
segments.text: He lay still and listened to the city coming awake outside his window, and waited for the morning bells.
